metadata.visit_title: Migraine follow-up — starting a preventive medication

transcript:
DR: Marisol, good to see you again. Last time we talked about how often the headaches were hitting — how have the last few weeks been?
PT: Honestly rough. I'm getting them maybe three, four times a week now. The ibuprofen barely touches it anymore.
DR: Three to four a week is a lot. And you're taking the ibuprofen most of those days?
PT: Pretty much. Some weeks every day.
DR: Okay. That's actually part of what I want to change, because taking a painkiller that often can start causing its own rebound headaches. So today I'd like to start you on something to prevent the migraines rather than just chase them.
PT: Prevent them, okay. What is it?
DR: It's called amitriptyline. It's an older medication, we use a low dose of it for migraine prevention. You'll take it at night because it can make you a little drowsy at first.
PT: At night, got it. How much?
DR: We start low. Ten milligrams every night for the first two weeks, and if you tolerate it fine we'll go up to twenty. Take it about an hour before bed.
PT: Ten milligrams, at night, then maybe twenty. Will it knock me out?
DR: The first week or so you might feel groggy in the morning — that usually settles. Don't drive if you feel foggy. Dry mouth is the other common one.
PT: Okay, I can live with that. Should I stop the ibuprofen?
DR: Cut it back — use it only for a bad breakthrough day, not daily. Let's aim for no more than twice a week.
PT: No more than twice a week. And how long until I know if it's working?
DR: Give it a solid six weeks before we judge it. Prevention meds take time to build up. Keep a little headache log — days you get one, how bad, what you took.
PT: I can do a log. My sister has migraines too, she said hers came with flashing lights before. I've never had that.
DR: Right, yours have always been without the visual aura, and that's stayed the same, which is good.
PT: One more thing — my jaw's been clicking, is that related?
DR: Probably separate, let's keep an eye on it and revisit if it gets painful. Let's book you back in six weeks.
PT: Sounds good. Thanks, doctor.
DR: Of course. The front desk will set up the follow-up.
PT: Great, see you then.
DR: Oh — Marisol, one important thing before you go: if you ever get a headache that's suddenly the worst one of your life, or it comes on like a thunderclap in seconds, or you get any weakness, trouble speaking, or vision loss with it — that's not a migraine, don't wait it out, go to the emergency room. Okay?
PT: Oh — okay. Worst ever or sudden, go in. Got it.
DR: Exactly. Take care.
note:
**Subjective:** Ms. Alvarez is a 34-year-old woman with chronic migraine without aura, here for follow-up. Reports increased frequency to 3–4 headaches per week with near-daily ibuprofen use and declining relief. No aura, no new neurologic features; pattern unchanged otherwise. Also notes intermittent jaw clicking, non-painful.

**Objective:** Well-appearing, no acute distress. Neurologic exam grossly nonfocal.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Chronic migraine without aura, increasing frequency with likely medication-overuse component. Start amitriptyline 10 mg nightly x2 weeks, then increase to 20 mg nightly if tolerated. Counseled on morning grogginess and dry mouth; advised not to drive if foggy.
- Reduce ibuprofen to no more than twice weekly for breakthrough only.
- Maintain a headache log (frequency, severity, treatment).
- Return precautions reviewed: sudden/thunderclap or worst-ever headache, or headache with focal neurologic deficit — seek emergency care.
- Follow-up in 6 weeks.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Chronic migraine, happening more often
• Starting a preventive medication

Next steps
• Start amitriptyline 10 mg at night for 2 weeks, then 20 mg at night if tolerated.
• Cut back ibuprofen to no more than twice a week.
• Keep a headache log.
• Follow up in 6 weeks.
